currency.name: Cambodian Riel
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: ៛40,000 (about £8). US dollars are accepted almost everywhere too.

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Cambodia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the south-east. It sits between Thailand, Laos and Vietnam 🌏

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Cambodia?
mapQuestions.answer: Phnom Penh. The city sits where four big rivers meet, including the Mekong.

mapQuestions.question: What is Cambodia's most famous ancient site?
mapQuestions.answer: Angkor Wat, the biggest religious building in the world. Built in the 1100s by the Khmer Empire.

mapQuestions.question: What huge lake is in the middle of Cambodia?
mapQuestions.answer: Tonle Sap. It is the biggest freshwater lake in South-East Asia. In the rainy season it swells to five times its dry-season size, and whole villages float on it.

mapQuestions.question: What language do Cambodians speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Khmer, written in a beautiful curly script. Cambodian people call themselves "Khmer".

phrases.nativeScript: ថ្លៃប៉ុន្មាន?
ខ្ញុំចង់បាន…

foods.name: Fish amok
foods.description: Cambodia's most famous dish: river fish steamed in coconut milk with lemongrass, turmeric and kaffir lime, traditionally served in a banana-leaf cup. Aromatic, mild, comforting.

foods.name: Lok lak
foods.description: Stir-fried beef cubes in a peppery soy sauce, served over rice and lettuce with a dip of lime juice, salt and Kampot pepper. The Cambodian comfort meal.

foods.name: Nom banh chok
foods.description: Cold rice noodles in a green fish-and-lemongrass curry, with a pile of fresh herbs and pickled vegetables on top. The classic Cambodian breakfast.

foods.name: Bai sach chrouk
foods.description: Grilled pork over broken rice, with pickled cucumber and a bowl of clear broth on the side. Eaten at sunrise from street stalls all over the country.

foods.name: Kuy teav
foods.description: A clear pork or beef noodle soup, with bean sprouts, herbs and crispy garlic. Cambodia's answer to pho, eaten morning to night.

foods.name: Kampot pepper
foods.description: Not a dish but Cambodia's most famous ingredient. Grown only in the Kampot region, it is considered the finest pepper in the world. Try it crushed fresh over your meal.

scavengerItems.label: A tuk-tuk weaving through traffic (Cambodian ones are bigger and breezier than Thai ones)
A piece of ancient temple stonework with a tree growing through it
School kids in white shirts and blue/black trousers cycling home
A shop using both US dollars AND Cambodian riel at the same time
A boat home floating on Tonle Sap lake or a river
An ox-cart on a country road
A monk in saffron robes collecting alms at sunrise
A rice paddy stretching to the horizon
A street vendor hacking open a coconut with a machete
A photo or painting of the king (Norodom Sihamoni) in a shop
A statue or carving of an apsara dancer with the famous curled fingers
A sign in Khmer script you cannot read at all (it has 33 consonants and 23 vowels)

geoMatches.description: The town next to Angkor Wat. Tree-shaded streets, a buzzing night market, and over 1,000 temples in the surrounding jungle.
The capital, on the Mekong. Royal palace with golden roofs, the National Museum full of Khmer statues, and a riverside that comes alive at sunset.
A quiet old colonial town with a famous "bamboo train", a flat wooden platform on wheels that flies along disused railway tracks at 30 km/h.
A sleepy river town in the south, surrounded by pepper farms and salt fields. Famous for the world's best pepper.
The huge inland lake. Whole floating villages of houses, schools, churches and shops sit on the water. Many families live their entire lives on a boat.
A still-wild jungle island off the southern coast. White-sand beaches, glowing plankton in the sea at night, and very few roads.

senses.smell: Lemongrass and coconut from a tagine of amok, jasmine offerings at a wat, charcoal smoke from grills, the Mekong river at dawn, monsoon rain on red dust…
senses.hear: "Suostei!", tuk-tuk engines, monks chanting at sunrise, the bamboo train clattering on broken rails, frogs in the rice paddies, monsoon rain on tin roofs…
senses.taste: Mild fragrant fish amok, peppery Kampot pepper, sweet palm sugar, salty fish sauce, juicy mango with chilli salt, cold beer (Angkor brand) in the heat…
senses.feel: Wet-season heat, the cool stone of an Angkor temple at dawn, rough red earth on bare feet, the wind on a tuk-tuk, the wobble of a floating village walkway…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is the biggest religious building in the world?
stories.body: Angkor Wat, in northern Cambodia. It was built around 1150, more than 870 years ago, by King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire, which back then ruled most of South-East Asia. The temple is so big that the whole of Vatican City could fit inside its outer walls. It started as a Hindu temple dedicated to the god Vishnu, but was converted to Buddhism a couple of hundred years later, and is still in use today. The carvings on every wall tell stories from old Hindu epics, with around 3,000 dancing "apsara" women, each one with a different face and hairstyle. The temple is now on the Cambodian flag, the only building on any national flag in the world.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Angkor took 30 years to build. What is a project you could start that might take a really long time to finish?

stories.question: What is it like to live on water?
stories.body: On Tonle Sap, the huge lake in the middle of Cambodia, around 80,000 people live in floating villages. Houses, schools, churches, mosques, basketball courts, and grocery shops all float on rafts or stilts. Kids paddle to school in their own little boats. The lake itself is amazing: in the dry season it shrinks to about 2,500 square km, but in the wet season the Mekong river flows BACKWARDS into it, filling it up to five times its dry size. Whole forests stand underwater for months. The flooded forest is one of the most productive fishing grounds in the world.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Would you rather live in a house that floats, a house in a tree, or a house in a tunnel under the ground?

stories.question: Who are the dancing women on every Cambodian wall?
stories.body: They are apsaras, celestial dancers from old Hindu mythology. Carved into the walls of Angkor and the other Khmer temples, there are thousands of them, each one slightly different. The Royal Ballet of Cambodia still performs apsara dance today, with the dancers wearing tall golden headdresses and gold-embroidered silk costumes. The dance is famous for its tiny, exact movements: every fingertip curves backwards into impossible shapes, every step is in slow motion, and every facial expression is held perfectly still. Dancers train for years to bend their fingers back like that, starting from very young.

stories.question: What was the Khmer Rouge?
stories.body: Between 1975 and 1979, a group called the Khmer Rouge ruled Cambodia. Their leader, Pol Pot, tried to turn the whole country into a giant farm. They emptied all the cities, forced everyone into the countryside, and banned money, schools, hospitals, religion and even glasses (because they suggested education). Around 1.7 million Cambodians (a quarter of the entire country) died from starvation, illness or violence in just under four years. It is one of the worst things any government has ever done to its own people. Today the Cambodian people are gentle and welcoming, but almost every family lost someone, and the country is still rebuilding.
stories.thinkingQuestion: What do you think helps a country and its people heal after something so terrible?

stories.question: Why is half the country still being slowly cleared of bombs?
stories.body: During the Vietnam War and the years that followed, Cambodia became one of the most heavily bombed countries on earth. Between 1965 and 1973, American planes dropped more than 2.7 million tonnes of bombs on Cambodia, much of it in secret. Many of those bombs (and the land mines later planted by the Khmer Rouge) never went off, and are still buried in the ground today. A British charity called the HALO Trust, and others, have been carefully clearing them for over 30 years. They have made huge progress, but tens of millions of explosives may still be out there. This is why visitors in rural Cambodia are always told to stick to marked paths.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Cambodia so far?
How does it feel to walk through a temple that is 900 years old?
What is one thing about Cambodian life that surprises you compared to home?
Have you found a favourite place here so far? What is it?
How do you handle the heat? Out of 10, how sweaty are you right now?
Cambodia is rebuilding from something terrible that happened only 50 years ago. What kind of country do you think Cambodia could become in another 50 years?

wordSearch.hint: Cambodian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Water buffalo
animals.description: Working every rice paddy across the country, often led by a small kid with a stick.

animals.name: Long-tailed macaque
animals.description: All over Angkor Wat. Curious, cheeky, will steal sunglasses.

animals.name: Tokay gecko
animals.description: Big chunky gecko that lives in houses. Their loud call is the soundtrack of the night.

animals.name: Asian elephant
animals.description: In the Mondulkiri sanctuary where rescued former working elephants now roam.

animals.name: Irrawaddy dolphin
animals.description: Rare snub-nosed freshwater dolphins in the Mekong River near Kratie.

animals.name: Giant ibis
animals.description: Cambodia's national bird — only 200 left in the wild, mostly in the Northern Plains.
